title: Windows BootLoader Inventory
spl:
`bootloader_inventory`
      | stats count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(_raw)
        BY host
      | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)`
      | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
      | `windows_bootloader_inventory_filter`
description: The following analytic identifies the bootloader paths on Windows endpoints. It leverages a PowerShell Scripted input to capture this data, which is then processed and aggregated using Splunk. Monitoring bootloader paths is significant for a SOC as it helps detect unauthorized modifications that could indicate bootkits or other persistent threats. If confirmed malicious, such activity could allow attackers to maintain persistence, bypass security controls, and potentially control the boot proces…

implementation: ESCU Hunting detection — not designed for automated alerting. Run ad-hoc or on a low-frequency schedule from Splunk Enterprise Security for analyst-driven investigation. Requires Various ingested and CIM-normalized.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes
  by Processes.dest Processes.process_name Processes.parent_process_name span=1h
| sort -count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration on Endpoint (Processes) before using this tstats search.
knownFalsePositives: Legitimate dev tools, installers, and system maintenance binaries can match; validate parent process, path, and signer.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"Windows BootLoader Inventory" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: Various. Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1542.001. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "Windows BootLoader Inventory" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: Legitimate dev tools, installers, and system maintenance binaries can match; validate parent process, path, and signer.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

Validation

After the search returns events, sample a few rows and compare the same host and time in your EDR console (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint portal, or Carbon Black) so usernames, process names, and dispositions line up. If Sysmon or Windows Security are listed in Data sources, open the Sysmon Operational log in Event Viewer and confirm the matching process, registry, or network line.
grandmaExplanation: We use endpoint security telemetry to stay ahead of the behavior this rule calls “Windows BootLoader Inventory”, so we catch real tradecraft and not just noise from everyday work.
